retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gingivitis is the most common of periodontal diseases. It is more prevalent in males than in females, as research has shown that females tend to follow better oral care regimes. It is commonly seen in children and adults. Studies have found gingivitis to be more prevalent in people with low socioeconomic status, as people with high socioeconomic status tend to show a more positive attitude towards the maintenance of oral hygiene. Also, they have better access to health care options. Studies reveal that gingivitis is more prevalent in pregnant women than in non-pregnant women. Moreover, more severe forms of gingivitis have been more often seen in pregnant women. [11] The most frequently seen types of gingivitis are plaque-induced, hormonal, acute ulcerative necrotizing, drug-induced, or spontaneously presenting hyperplastic gingivitis. Categorically, the more predominant form of gingivitis is plaque-induced. This type accounts for far more cases than all other variants combined. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: During pregnancy, there are not only changes in hormone levels but also a greater predisposition to dilating blood vessels. These factors contribute to an exaggerated inflammatory response by the gingival tissues, even to a minor quantity of plaque accumulation. It has been suggested that the levels of estrogen determine the severity of gingival inflammation created against the biofilm at the gingival margin. [7] [8] The hormonal alterations that occur during puberty influence how the gingival tissue reacts to plaque accumulation, causing what is known as puberty gingivitis. It has been found that in the cytoplasm of the cells of the gingiva, receptors for both estrogens and testosterone that have a high affinity for these hormones are present. The receptors for estrogen are specifically present in the basal and spinous layers of the epithelium. In the connective tissue, such receptors are found in the fibroblasts and endothelial cells of small vessels. Therefore, the gingiva is an easy target organ for these steroid hormones, resulting in gingivitis. It has been observed that during adolescence, gingivitis appears earlier in girls (11 to 13 years) than in boys (13 to 14 years). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periodontium -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Epidemiological studies have shown that periodontal diseases are associated with an increased risk of preterm birth and low birth weight (LBW). This can happen by direct vertical transfer of maternal bacteria that cause inflammation (Gram-negative bacteria in the dental plaque biofilm, such as Porphyromonas gingivalis and Fusobacterium nucleatum. [11] It is also believed that the release of inflammatory cytokines, specifically IL-lα, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8 and systemic inflammation, contributes to preterm birth and LBW. This emphasizes the importance of oral hygiene measurements and preventions during pregnancy. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Gingiva -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of periodontal disease can be attributed to several factors, which can be categorized as modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors. Modifiable risk factors include tobacco smoking, poor oral hygiene, diabetes, and pregnancy. One of the most important modifiable risk factors is tobacco smoking, as the risk for periodontal disease is 5 to 20 times higher in people who smoke compared to those who do not smoke. [17] Perhaps the most noteworthy risk factor is poor oral hygiene; as bacteria and plaque build-up in the tight spaces between the gingival mucosa and surface of the tooth, host inflammatory processes involved in defending against infection end up destroying bone and tissue. Diabetes contributes to gingivitis and periodontal disease by impairing wound healing, which can lead to gingival breakdown. [18] Elevated estrogen levels during pregnancy positively correlate with Porphyromonas gingivalis , a key microbe involved in the pathogenesis of gingivitis. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hormonal fluctuations during pregnancy can enhance inflammatory responses, contributing to the development of gingivitis and periodontitis. Although the exact mechanisms are not fully understood, maternal hormone levels have been linked to P gingivalis , which is a key pathogen in periodontal disease progression. Both hypoestrogenism and hyperestrogenism. [17] [18] [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Necrotizing Ulcerative Gingivitis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Healthy gums are extremely important to a patient's overall health. Maintaining good oral hygiene is the most effective way to avoid developing destructive gum disease such as gingivitis. There are many steps to take to keep gums healthy, including twice-daily brushing, daily flossing or interdental cleaning, and visiting a dentist regularly. [11] Health conditions like HIV infection, diabetes, and cancer can reduce a person's ability to fight infection, which can increase the risk of developing gum disease. Other factors that can affect the gums are medications, such as anti-seizure medications and some blood pressure medications, hormonal changes, and the use of tobacco. Thus, it is very important for pregnant patients and patients meeting any of these criteria to see a dentist regularly. [11] Keeping gums healthy and preventing disease is possible with daily care and regular visits to a dentist. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Another significant complication associated with periodontal disease is the increased risk of preterm low birth weight infants. Research has shown a strong correlation between maternal periodontal disease and reduced infant birth weight. As the severity of periodontal disease increases, from none to severe, infant birth weight tends to decrease, according to prior studies. [65] [66] [67] [68] [69]

retrieved_docs.content: 2. Nutritional gingivitis

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal disease arises from multiple factors, including patient-specific risk factors and inadequate oral hygiene. These risk factors are categorized as modifiable (such as smoking, poor oral hygiene, diabetes mellitus, and pregnancy) or non-modifiable (including age, heredity, and genetic disorders). Poor oral hygiene is a major contributor, as plaque buildup correlates with increased disease severity and prevalence. Anaerobic bacteria, including Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans , Porphyromonas gingivalis , Treponema denticola , and Tannerella forsythia , colonize deep within periodontal pockets, thereby triggering inflammation through host immune responses. [1] [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses for DIGO include the following: False enlargement of gingival tissue: Pseudo-enlargement of the gingiva caused by an enlarged underlying bony tissue. The gingiva here is normal. Inflammation: Chronically inflamed gingival tissue is red or violaceous, smooth, and tends to bleed to the touch. Familial or hereditary conditions: Examples include familial fibromatosis, idiopathic fibromatosis, gingivomatosis, and hereditary gingival hyperplasia. The gingiva is pink and non-tender, involves the attached gingiva, the gingival margin, and the interdental papillae, and has a firm and leathery consistency. Physiological states: Puberty and pregnancy are associated with gingival enlargement. Scurvy: Vitamin C deficiency can produce very tender bleeding gingiva. Systemic diseases: leukemia, tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, as their diagnosis can be corroborated hematologically.

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology and pathogenesis of gingivitis. Assess the clinical signs and symptoms of gingivitis. Differentiate the treatment options for patients with gingivitis. Outline the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients affected by gingivitis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth loss is one of the most serious consequences of periodontal disease, which occurs as the disease progresses and damages the periodontium that anchors the teeth. Beyond the oral tissues, periodontal disease is also associated with systemic conditions, including diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, and pregnancy complications, such as preterm birth and low-birth-weight infants. [53] Therefore, integrating systemic health considerations into periodontal treatment is essential for optimal patient care. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Appendicitis in Pregnancy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiologies of acute appendicitis in pregnant patients are similar to those in nonpregnant adults. [9] Specific microorganisms such as Fusobacterium nucleatum , an anaerobic oral commensal and periodontal pathogen, are associated with pregnancy-related complications such as chorioamnionitis, preterm birth, stillbirth, neonatal sepsis, and preeclampsia. However, the evidence for distinct microbiological culprits for acute appendicitis during pregnancy is lacking. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Syphilis is caused by a spirochete known as Treponema pallidum that can be sexually transmitted (vaginal, anal, or oral contact) or passed through the placenta, causing congenital syphilis. It has an incubation period of approximately 20 to 40 days. The host for T. pallidum is humans, and it has no animal reservoir. [5] Gonorrhea

retrieved_docs.content: 3. Established lesion

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Various drugs used for systemic conditions can cause gingivitis as a side effect, such as phenytoin (used for epileptic seizures), calcium channel blockers (used for angina, high blood pressure), anticoagulants, fibrinolytic agents, oral contraceptive agents, protease inhibitors, vitamin A, and analogs. The mechanism behind this gingival inflammation is thought to be the ability of the metabolites of these drugs to induce the proliferation of fibroblasts. An imbalance between the synthesis and the degradation of the extracellular matrix leads to the accumulation of immature proteins in the extracellular matrix, particularly collagen. This, in turn, results in gingivitis. [10] Apart from the already mentioned, various risk and influencing factors can contribute to the development of gingivitis. These include smoking and tobacco chewing, systemic conditions, genetic factors (hereditary gingival fibromatosis), and local conditions (dry mouth, crowded teeth).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified conditions related to pregnancy comprise a range of distinct gestational disorders. This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: Infectious process is identified as a pathological mechanism underlying post‑abortion complications, and these complications are referred to in Arabic as “إجْهاضٌ مَصْحُوبٌ بعَدْوَى.” This relationship highlights that infection can be a key contributor to adverse outcomes following an abortion, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and managing infectious etiologies to prevent or treat post‑abortion complications.

This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

The infectious process is identified as the underlying mechanism of post‑abortion complications, linking infection directly to adverse outcomes after termination procedures. Collectively, this structured framework interrelates prenatal surveillance, invasive diagnostics, therapeutic interventions, delivery assistance, placental and ectopic‑pregnancy management, and post‑delivery care, providing a comprehensive reference for clinical documentation, education, and patient‑centered obstetric management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical insufficiency, postpartum sepsis, retained placenta or membranes without hemorrhage, prolonged pregnancy, other previous delivery, unspecified early‑pregnancy bleeding, and malignant tumor of the placenta are all classified as members of the “Complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor and delivery (excluding miscarriage and stillbirth) (SMQ).” This SMQ category groups together a range of obstetric conditions that represent significant complications or risk factors during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period, emphasizing their importance for clinical assessment, monitoring, and management in maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2141
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with both الولادة القيصرية (Cesarean delivery) and الالتهاب بطانة الرحم (endometritis), indicating that pregnancies occurring within the uterine cavity may increase the likelihood of requiring a surgical birth and are linked to inflammation of the uterine lining. Recognizing these associations is important for anticipating delivery method decisions and for monitoring and managing potential infectious complications in pregnant patients.

The overarching concept **LABOR AND PARTURITION** is parented by **threatened labor** (“مخاض مهدد”), reinforcing that early or threatened labor is identified as threatened labor. Collectively, these interrelated concepts delineate how cervical changes, bleeding, placental disorders, hypertensive and circulatory diseases, molar pregnancies, infectious miscarriages, and abnormal delivery findings are organized within the clinical framework of pregnancy, labor, and postpartum care, supporting diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of pregnant patients.
